utterances: …come to the U.S.
for education.
Noboru, thanks very much for the call and good luck.
Thanks for taking me.
We're talking about legal immigration and who should get in.
Our guests are Jeanne Butterfield and Tamar Jacoby.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And if you'd like to look at a map of global migration and read about immigration myths, you can go to npr.org.
And let's get Serena(ph) on the line.
Miss Serena is with us from Quakerstown, Pennsylvania.
Hi.
Hi.
I love your show.
Thank you.
I am very interested in this issue because I'm a (unintelligible) staff of the Lutheran Church in this area.
And the Lutheran Church has been very active in immigration resettlement.
And in our own community, we have some - what you would call lost boys, who are now men, living in the area and came as orphans, believed that there was no family left.
And now, are discovering that there are family members, who, because of the war and the things that put them into refugee status, they are now discovering that they do have family.
But under this new system, it would be very difficult for them to bring those family members here…
That's…
…and they have been here.
Let's get a response from Tamar Jacoby.
Yeah.
I mean, look, you know, obviously the human stories are heart wrenching.
I mean, who wants to have to say, well, you can't bring your sibling.

But this is a limited resource and we need to be a competitive country and we don't have to be heartless and cruel about it to say that not every heart, I'm sorry, but not every heart-wrenching story deserves a visa.
Well, it's not only about those stories, it's also about the difficulty with the resources it takes to support them or individuals or nuclear families who are trying to resettle here.
Right.
If there's a third adult in the household…
Right.
…that provides an incredible amount of stability…
And that's…
…in terms of babysitting, in terms of income, in terms of all kinds of things.
Jeanne Butterfield.
And that's - I totally agree with you.
And that's exactly my point that who can evaluate the value of that sibling to the family, to the economy, to the society.

And to say that a sibling can come in as a Ph.D., but someone who's coming to support the small business or take care of the children or whatever cannot, I mean, I think we still need to protect and preserve some number albeit within limits of those family relationships.
All right.
Thanks very much for the call, Serena.
Thank you.
Here's an email, finally, from Mark(ph) in San Francisco.
The poem at the base of the Statue of Liberty written by Emma Lazarus, a daughter of poor, uneducated immigrant, says give me your poor, your homeless, your huddled masses yearning to be free.
Should we change that to give me your skilled and educated?
The law of unintended consequences is a powerful thing.
We can't social engineer the future.
Now, that's a fair question and that's why I think it's very important…
Tamar Jacoby.
(Singing) No time to count what I'm worth 'cause I just left the planet Earth.
Where I go I'll hope there's rum...
…that we tweak the point system so that it isn't just the skilled and educated.
I strongly agree that we need every year a half a million additional unskilled workers or we can't grow the economy.
That's what supply and demand has been generating.
That's how we've grown in the past decades.
We need those unskilled workers.
And some will want to go home but we do want to keep the ones who are hardworking and enterprising and take here and fall in love with America.
And so, you know, it's a totally fair question.
But I think with some modest tweaks to the proposed point system that it can work for those people as well.
And let's turn to you Jeanne Butterfield.
For much of 20th century, huddled masses - this wasn't a question - very few immigrants were let into the country.
Right.
We have a very robust and generous immigration system, there's no doubt.
And I think within that, we need to be respectful of both family relationships, which have made America an immigrant success story.
And let's protect and preserve that as well as looking at the needs of our economy going forward and tweaking an employment-based system and perhaps experimenting with the point system.
Jeanne Butterfield, thanks very much for being with us today.
Thank you.

Tamar Jacoby, we thank you for your time as well.

Decades from now when someone decides to check records to see who won the women's 100 meters of the 2000 Olympic Games in Sydney, the entry may read, question mark.
Olympic officials announced this week they are considering leaving the gold medal winner empty.
Marion Jones, who appeared to win, has confessed she used performance-enhancing drugs.
But the silver medalist, Greek sprinter Katerini Thanou, was involved in a doping scandal during the 2004 games in Athens.
So the IOC is reluctant to offer her the consolation prize.
IOC board member Denis Oswald said we'll see how insistent Thanou is.
The best way would be not to do anything and just leave it as it is, and there is no gold.
Well, they could give it to Barry Bonds.
This is WEEKEND EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Scott Simon.
In Washington, D.C., Labor Day weekend marks the end of the August doldrums, the beginning of a busy fall season for politics in government.
